title: Decision Boundaries
concept: A decision boundary is the cutoff line (usually 0.5 probability) that divides a model's predictions into yes or no classes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does a logistic regression model find the best fitting S-curve?

options.text: By using gradient descent to iteratively update weights and minimize log loss
By drawing a straight line directly through all the points
By calculating a perfect closed-form formula in one step

exercises.explain: Exactly! Logistic regression uses gradient descent to bend the S-curve until log loss is minimized. Now, let's see how we use this curve to make actual binary choices.

cards.title: Filtering spam
cards.body: Your email filter gets a spam probability of 51%. Does it send it to spam? Yes. 49%? No. Even though the probabilities are close, a hard decision must be made.

cards.title: Decision boundary, defined
cards.body: A decision boundary is the cutoff line (or threshold) that divides predictions into distinct classes. By default, any probability of 0.5 or higher is classified as 'yes' (1), and anything below is 'no' (0).

cards.title: The height check
cards.body: Think of a movie theater's height check. If you're 48 inches or taller, you can ride the roller coaster; if you're 47.9 inches, you can't. The 48-inch mark is the decision boundary.

cards.title: Age vs heart rate
cards.body: In a 2D graph predicting if a patient is healthy or sick based on age and heart rate, the decision boundary is the line where the model's output is exactly 0.5. On one side of the line is healthy, on the other is sick.

cards.title: Myth: The cutoff is always 0.5
cards.body: False. You can shift the boundary! For rare medical conditions, we might set the boundary to 0.2 to catch every possible case, accepting more false alarms.

cards.title: Comparing thresholds
cards.body: Two ways a decision boundary can be adjusted based on needs:

right.text:
Catch dangerous events early
Predicts class 1 if probability ≥ 20%

exercises.prompt: What does a decision boundary represent in a classification model?

options.text: The threshold where the model's output changes from one class to another
The total number of errors the model made
The average value of the input features
The learning rate of gradient descent

exercises.explain: The decision boundary is the threshold (like 0.5 probability) where the model's prediction flips from 'no' to 'yes'.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A decision boundary must always be set at exactly 0.5 probability.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! You can adjust the threshold depending on the cost of making different types of mistakes, like catching a rare disease.

exercises.sentence: If a spam filter uses a default threshold of 0.5, an email with a 0.72 probability of being spam will be classified as ___.
exercises.explain: Since 0.72 is greater than or equal to 0.5, the model makes the hard decision to classify it as spam.

exercises.prompt: Which model is using a custom decision boundary to prioritize safety?

options.text: A medical scanner that flags a scan if there is even a 15% chance of a tumor
An email filter that sends emails to spam only if they are 100% guaranteed to be spam
A weather app that predicts rain if the probability is exactly 50%

exercises.explain: By setting a low threshold of 15% (0.15), the medical scanner prioritizes catching tumors early, even if it creates more false alarms.

exercises.prompt: Where does a linear model's decision boundary exist mathematically on the S-curve?

options.text: Where the calculated probability is exactly 0.5
At the very top of the S-curve (probability 1.0)
At the very bottom of the S-curve (probability 0.0)
At the average value of the weights

exercises.explain: The decision boundary is the point where the logistic function (S-curve) outputs exactly 0.5. At this point, the model is perfectly undecided.

exercises.prompt: You are building a fraud detector for a bank. Missing a fraudulent transaction is extremely costly, but a false alarm just requires a quick verification. How should you adjust your decision boundary?

options.text: Lower the threshold (e.g., to 0.2) to flag transactions even with lower probability of fraud
Raise the threshold (e.g., to 0.8) to only flag transactions that are almost certainly fraud
Keep the threshold at exactly 0.5, because standard settings are always optimal
Disable the decision boundary entirely and output raw probability numbers to customers

exercises.explain: Exactly! Lowering the threshold means you catch more fraud cases (fewer false negatives), which is worth the minor inconvenience of extra verification calls. You've mastered decision thresholds!
